--- Page 1 ---
1
Joint Access Point Activation and Power Allocation for
Cell-Free Massive MIMO Aided ISAC Systems
Nguyen Xuan Tung, Le Tung Giang, Trinh Van Chien, Hoang Trong Minh, Lajos Hanzo, Life Fellow, IEEE
Abstract—Cell-free
massive
multiple-input
multiple-output
(MIMO)-aided integrated sensing and communication (ISAC)
systems are investigated where distributed access points jointly
serve users and sensing targets. We demonstrate that only a
subset of access points (APs) has to be activated for both tasks,
while deactivating redundant APs is essential for power savings.
This motivates joint active AP selection and power control for
optimizing energy efficiency. The resultant problem is a mixed-
integer nonlinear program (MINLP). To address this, we propose
a model-based Branch-and-Bound approach as a strong baseline
to guide a semi-supervised heterogeneous graph neural network
(HetGNN) for selecting the best active APs and the power
allocation. Comprehensive numerical results demonstrate that the
proposed HetGNN reduces power consumption by 20 −25% and
runs nearly 10, 000 times faster than model-based benchmarks.
Index Terms—Cell-free, ISAC, joint radar and communication,
graph neural network, power allocation.
I. INTRODUCTION
Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) services are
vital for next-generation wireless networks, enabling spectrum
and hardware reuse at a modest added cost [1]. Sensing in
ISAC also enhances communication via improved localization,
resource allocation, and CSI tracking. For fully harnessing
these benefits, numerous studies have been conducted to
optimize beamforming [2], radar signal processing [3], and
resource allocation [4]. Early ISAC research primarily aimed
for single integrated dual-function at transmitters, categorized
into single-antenna systems leveraging waveform diversity
[3] and multi-antenna systems optimizing beamforming [2].
Recent research has then also evolved to multi-transmitter
ISAC systems [5], [6], where distributed transmitters and
receivers collaborate under a Cloud Radio Access Network
(C-RAN) relying on centralized management.
An emerging topic is that of integrating sensing into cell-
free massive MIMO (cf-mMIMO) systems to leverage its
distributed access points (AP) architecture for enhancing cov-
L. Hanzo would like to acknowledge the financial support of the Engineer-
ing and Physical Sciences Research Council (EPSRC) projects under grant
EP/Y026721/1, EP/W032635/1 and EP/X04047X/1 as well as of the European
Research Council’s Advanced Fellow Grant QuantCom (Grant No. 789028).
(Corresponding author: Nguyen Xuan Tung.)
Nguyen Xuan Tung is with Faculty of Interdisciplinary Digital Technology,
PHENIKAA University, Yen Nghia, Ha Dong, Hanoi 12116, Viet Nam (e-
mail: tung.nguyenxuan@phenikaa-uni.edu.vn). Le Tung Giang is with the De-
partment of Information Convergence Engineering, Pusan National University,
Busan 46241, Republic of Korea (email: giang.lt2399144@pusan.ac.kr). Trinh
Van Chien is with the School of Information and Communication Technology
(SoICT), Hanoi University of Science and Technology (HUST), Vietnam
(email: chientv@soict.hust.edu.vn). Trong-Minh Hoang is with the Posts and
Telecommunications Institute of Technology, Vietnam (e-mail: hoangtrong-
minh@ptit.edu.vn). Lajos Hanzo is with the Department of Electronics and
Computer Science, University of Southampton, Southampton SO17 1BJ, U.K.
(e-mail: lh@ecs.soton.ac.uk).
erage, spatial diversity, and target detection accuracy [7].
However, optimizing ISAC in cf-mMIMO systems presents
challenges due to the escalating system complexity. Con-
ventional methods decouple radar sensing and communica-
tion, leading to suboptimal resource utilization and scalability
issues [6]. Moreover, the computational overhead becomes
prohibitive in large-scale networks. Recent AI-driven ISAC
studies confirm that deep learning (DL)-based approaches are
capable of addressing these issues, significantly improving
both sensing accuracy and resource allocation efficiency [8].
However, they often lack generalization to scenarios beyond
their training configuration. Graph neural networks (GNNs)
have emerged as a promising solution, modeling complex
relationships and ensuring scalability by generalizing across
network configurations [9]–[11]. By representing a wireless
system as a graph, where nodes and edges capture system en-
tities and interactions, GNNs succeed in predicting outcomes
based on node and edge features rather than fixed system
dimensions. Hence, they are well-suited for dynamic wireless
environments.
Against this backdrop, we propose a novel heterogeneous
GNN (HetGNN) for resource allocation in a cell-free mas-
sive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO)-based C-RAN,
where dual-function APs serve users and perform sensing
by cooperating with receiving stations (SRs) via the C-RAN
controller. The goal is to minimize total power consumption
while meeting the UE’s data throughput and sensing accuracy
requirement under the Cramer-Rao bound (CRB). We will
demonstrate that activating all APs, as in [12], leads to
unnecessary service and resource wastage. Explicitly, utilizing
only a necessary subset is more efficient, especially since
the AP locations directly impact power allocation, hence
affecting both communication and sensing. This motivates
the joint optimization of AP selection and power control. To
the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to jointly
optimize both the AP activation and power allocation for
both communication and sensing. Given the mixed-integer
nonlinear (MINL) nature of the problem, we model the ISAC
system as a heterogeneous graph and harness a HetGNN
for learning both APs activation and power control strategies
while ensuring constraint satisfaction. The semi-supervised
HetGNN conceived is trained using guidance from a pro-
posed model-based Branch-and-Bound approach. Numerical
results demonstrate that the designed HetGNN outperforms
optimization-based methods, achieving significantly reduced
power consumption and prompt execution.
Notation: Boldface lowercase letters and boldface uppercase
letters denote vectors and matrices, respectively. Let ()T and
()∗denote the transpose and conjugate, respectively. The
arXiv:2507.09425v1  [cs.IT]  12 Jul 2025


--- Page 2 ---
2
Euclidean norm and expectation are denoted by ∥·∥, and E{·}.
Finally, we define the circularly symmetric complex Gaussian
distribution with variance σ2 by CN(0, σ2).
II. INTEGRATED SENSING AND DOWNLINK
CELLFREE-MASSIVE MIMO COMMUNICATION
A. System and Signal Models
We consider a cf-mMIMO ISAC system where a set of
UEs, K = {1, ..., K}, are jointly served by a set of dual-
function APs, M = {1, ..., M}, as illustrated in Fig. 1. Each
AP sends a combined signal to all UEs in support of efficient
resource utilization. The downlink cf-mMIMO communication
signal is exploited for localizing a single-point target vehicle
within the area, facilitating further use in future applications.
The signals transmitted from all APs are reflected by the
sensing target, e.g., a vehicle, and are collected by a set
T
= {1, ..., T} of sensing receivers (SRs). Following the
passive sensing philosophy, each SR features two signals:
the target’s echoed signal and the reference signals received
from APs. By comparing these signals, key target parameters
such as the location, speed, and Angle of Arrival (AoA)
are estimated [13]. For simplicity, we focus our attention on
the accuracy of target localization. All the M APs and T
SRs are connected to the C-RAN controller [14] for resource
management and signal processing.
1) Communication model
We first consider the communication between APs and UEs,
in the uplink pilot training phase, when each AP estimates the
propagation channels impinging upon all UEs. The minimum
mean-square error estimate (MMSE) of the channel between
the k-th AP and the n-th UE is given by
ˆhmk =
E{˜y∗
p,mkhmk}
E{|˜yp,mk|2} ˜yp,mk
=
√τpρpςmk
τpρp
P
k′∈K
ςmk′|θH
k θk′|2 + σ2
UL
˜yp,mk,
(1)
where ˜yp,mk = θH
n yp,m, and yp,m = √τpρp
P
k∈K
hmkθk +
ξp,m is the pilot signal received at the k-th AP.
Here,
hmk ∼CN(0, ςmk) and ςmk denote the channel coefficient
and the large-scale fading between the m-th AP and the
k-th UE. Meanwhile, τp, ρp, and θk, and ξp,m are the
uplink training duration, the transmit pilot power, the pilot
sequence used by the k-th UE, and the additive noise at the
m-th AP, respectively. The channel estimate is distributed
as ˆhmk ∼CN(0, vmk), in which vmk ≜E{|ˆhmk|2} =
τpρpς2
mk
τpρp
P
k′∈K ςmk|θH
k θk′|2+1.
Note that the UEs are distributed heterogeneously, hence,
their throughput directly depends on their downlink signal
quality gleaned from nearby APs, rather than from distant
APs. Thus, activating only a subset Mact of APs may suffice
for satisfying the UEs’ requirement, despite turning off the
remaining APs for conserving energy. Given the channel
estimate obtained from the pilot training phase, the m-th AP
transmits the combined signal, xm = P
k∈K
√Pmkˆh∗
mksk, to the
UEs. Here, sk with E

|sk|2	
= 1 and Pmk are the symbol
intended for the k-th UE and the corresponding allocated
CPU
AP
JRC-SR
UE
fronthaul link
Target
reference signal
echoed signal
Fig. 1: Illustration of the cf-mMIMO ISAC system.
power, respectively. Accordingly, the signal received at the
k-th UE is given by
yd,k =
X
m∈Mact
hmksk + ξd,k
= √ρd
X
m∈Mact
X
k′∈K
p
Pmk′hmkˆh∗
mk′sk′ + ξd,k,
(2)
where Pmk′ represents the downlink (DL) transmission power
allocated by the m-th AP to transmit the symbol sk′. Here,
ξd,k ∼CN (0, 1) is the additive noise at the k-th UE. Note that
the desired signal sk is detected from yd,k in (2). The closed-
form expression of the Signal-to-Noise-Ratio (SINR) of the
k-th user is adopted from [14], as expressed in (3), where σ2
DL
denotes the noise power normalized by the downlink transmit
power ρDL.
2) Sensing model
In the sensing context, a cross-ambiguity function is calcu-
lated between the reference signal and the target echoed signal
[15]. Let APs and SRs be located in a 2-D coordinate system
as [xAP
m , yAP
m ] and [xSR
t , ySR
t ], respectively. The coordinates of
the target are denoted by [x, y]. Then, the distance from the
m-th AP to the target is RAP
m =
p
(xAP
m −x)2 + (yAP
m −y)2.
Similarly, the distance from the t-th SR to the target is
RSR
t
=
p
(xSR
t
−x)2 + (ySR
t
−y)2. The propagation delay
from the m-th AP to the t-th SR is ∆tmt = RAP
m+RSR
t
c
, where
c = 3 × 108[m/s] is the propagation velocity of the signal. At
the time l, the signal received at the m-th SR is expressed as:
rt(l) =
X
m∈M
χmt
p
Pksk(l −∆tmt) + zt(t0),
(4)
where zt(l) ∼CN(0, σ2
S) is the AWGN at the t-th SR, the
coefficient χmt represents the effect of the target radar cross
section (RCS), which are independent from each other.
For any unbiased (or asymptotically unbiased) estimator of
unknown parameter(s), the CRB provides a lower bound on
the mean-square error (MSE) of target location estimation.
This bound is commonly referred to as the Cram´er-Rao Lower
Bound (CRLB) and is derived in [13]. Accordingly, CRLB’s
matrix representation for target location is defined as:
Cx,y(psen) =
( X
m∈Mact
Psen,m


qcm
qcm
qbm
)−1
,
(5)
where

=

with

and Psen,m

target location is expressed as:
σ2
x,y(psen) =
bTpsen
pTsenApsen
,
(6)
where
b = qa + qb,
A = qaqT
b −qcqT
c ,
(7)
with qa = [ga1, · · · , gaM ]T, qb = [gb1, · · · , gbM ]T, and qc =
[gc1, · · · , gcM ]T. The target location is updated in real time to
determine the localization accuracy at each time step.
B. Problem Formulation
Our objective is to minimize the total transmit power across
all RRUs while simultaneously meeting both the communica-
tion throughput and the sensing accuracy requirements. The
optimization problem is mathematically formulated as follows:
minimization
psen,Mact
X
m∈Mact
X
k∈K
Pmk +
X
m∈Mact
P cir
(8a)
s.t
X
k∈K
vmkPmk ≤1, ∀m ∈M,
(8b)
γk ≥γthr, ∀k ∈K,
(8c)
bT psen −νpT
senApsen ≤0.
(8d)
Here, P cir denotes the power required to activate and maintain
a single AP for the cf-mMIMO DL communication. Con-
straint (8b) limits the power budget, (8c) ensures meeting the
minimum SINR for UEs, and (8d) enforces the CRLB-based
sensing accuracy with threshold ν.
With a fixed set of active APs, the power allocation problem
becomes convex by relaxing constraint (8d) and reformulating
(8), allowing efficient solutions via tools like CVX [16].
However, determining the optimal set of active APs involves
evaluating a total of
M
P
i=1
M!
i!(M−i)! configurations. This makes
exhaustive search infeasible, particularly in large-scale net-
works. While heuristic approaches can accelerate the search
process, they often yield suboptimal solutions. This inspires
the search for a more efficient solution having improved
performance, yet reduced complexity.
III. HETEROGENEOUS GNN-BASED APPROACH
In this section, we introduce a novel learning-based frame-
work, referred to as the HetGNN-based model, to address the
power allocation problem in (8). Specifically, we first propose
a modified Branch-and-Bound (modified-BB) technique for
determining a set of active APs and simultaneously allocate
power. The modified-BB approach generates labels to guide
Algorithm 1 AP Activation and Power Allocation via Branch-
and-Bound with Relaxed Constraints
1: Input: System parameters M, K, and T .
2: Initialize the BB tree with all APs active, Mact = M.
3: Solve (9) and calculate P curr
tot
=
P
m∈Mact
P
k∈K
Pmk. Set the
level counter i = 0.
4: repeat
5:
i = i + 1.
6:
for each AP m′ ∈Mact do
7:
Set Mtemp
act
= Mact \ {m′}.
8:
Solve the power allocation problem in (9) for Mtemp
act .
Calculate P temp
tot
=
P
m∈Mact
P
k∈K
Pmk.
9:
if Problem is infeasible then
10:
Prune the branch.
11:
else
12:
if P temp
tot
≤P curr
tot
then
13:
Retain the branch for further exploration.
14:
end if
15:
end if
16:
end for
17: until No nodes are pruned.
18: Output:Best solution found.
the semi-supervised training of the proposed HetGNN model,
using a small weighting coefficient.
A. Modified-BB Algorithm
We construct a Branch-and-Bound (BB) tree that begins
with all APs activated. We assume that the problem in (8)
is feasible. At the first level of the BB tree, the system
iteratively removes an AP k, forming a new active AP set
Mtemp
act
= M \ {k}. The problem in (8) is then solved for
this updated set Mtemp
act . To find solution for problem (8),
following [13], we first relax the non-convex constraint (8d)
as b −νApsen ≤0. This relaxation results in a convex
CRLB constraint and guarantees that the minimum transmit
power is close or equal to the true optimum of problem (8).
Furthermore, we introduce an auxiliary variable. Then we can
reformulate the problem in (8) as
minimization
psen,{ymk}
X
m∈Mtemp
act
X
k∈K
y2
mk
(9a)
s.t
Pmk ≥y2
mk, ∀m, k
(9b)
(8b), (10),
(9c)
b −νApsen ≤0.
(9d)
The constraint in (10) is reformulated from (8c), as illustrated
at the top of the page. The optimization problem in (9) is now
in convex form and can be efficiently solved using the CVX
toolbox [16]. If the problem is infeasible, the branch is pruned,
as the corresponding active AP set is not viable. Otherwise,


--- Page 4 ---
4
p
γthr
v
u
u
t
X
k′̸=k,k′∈K
 X
m∈M
ymk′vmk
ςmk
ςmk′
!2
+
X
k′∈K
X
m∈M
y2
mk′vmk′ςmk + σ2
DL ≤
X
m∈M
ymkvmk, ∀k ∈K
(10)
cf-mMIMO ISAC
HetGraph
Graph
Modeling
Final Embedding
Final edge
attribute
HetGNN
Power Control Policy
HetGNN Layers
 UE, AP, SR   
  Nodes Update
 Edges Update
Fig. 2: The general flow chart of the training phase.
the set Mtemp
act
is kept, if the total power consumption of the
temporary structure is smaller than the previous structure. The
process continues recursively, removing and evaluating APs
at each level until no further APs can be removed without
violating feasibility constraints. The detailed procedure of the
modified BB technique is summarized in Algorithm 1.
Remark 1: By setting the condition in the 13-th step, the
modified BB algorithm prunes the BB tree, retaining only a
suffice branch per level. This reduces the search space and
lowers its complexity to O(M 2). This is a clear improvement
compared to the case keeping all possible branches associated
with the complexity of O(M 3). This reduction is crucial since
the inner loop solves (9) having 2MK optimization variables
and MK + M + K constraints using CVX in each iteration,
resulting in a complexity of O[(2MK)3]. The modified ap-
proach significantly reduces the overall computational burden.
♢
B. HetGNN-based Model
The proposed HetGNN-based model consists of three main
steps: (i) graph modeling, (ii) graph convolutional layer, and
(iii) downstream task, as illustrated in Fig 2.
1) Graph Modeling the cf-mMIMO ISAC System
To enable GNN-based power control, we represent the
ISAC system as a heterogeneous graph (HetGraph) G =
⟨VAP, VUE, VSR, Ecomm, Erad⟩, where VAP, VUE, and VSR denote
the sets of AP, UE, and SR nodes, respectively. The sets
Ecomm and Erad represent the communication and radar edges.
Each AP node m is initialized with its location [xAP
m , yAP
m ],
sensing parameters [qam, qbm, qcm], SINR threshold γthr
m , and
localization accuracy requirement ν. The UE and SR nodes
are initialized without features. Each communication edge
ecomm
mk
∈Ecomm connects AP m to UE k, with the channel
gain gmk as its initial feature. Each radar edge erad
mt ∈Erad
connects AP m to SR t, with the radar cross-section (RCS)
coefficient ξmt as its initial feature.
2) HetGNN with Node/Edge Convolutional Layers
To capture information from all the edges and nodes in the
graph, we design the three steps of the edge convolutional
layer in the HetGNN as hereafter. Note that the superscript
(d) denotes the (d)-th layer, while the superscript X, and
Y represent the type of node and edge, respectively. Here,
we have X ∈{AP, SR, UE}, and Y ∈{comm, radar}. The
forward computation during each edge convolutional layer is
as follows:
Aggregation: Messages from the neighboring nodes are
aggregated as follows:
m(vX,(d)
i
) = ΘX,(d)
vj∈A(vX
i )

ψX,(d) 
v(d−1)
j
, eY,(d)
ij

,
(11)
where A(vX
i ) is the set of adjacent neighbors of node vX
i ,
vX,(d)
i
is the hidden representation of node vX
i , ψX,(d) and
ΘX,(d) are the MLP and pooling functions for the X node
type, respectively.
Node Update: The node updates its features as:
vX,(d)
i
= ϕX,(d) 
vX,(d−1)
i
, m(vX,(d)
i
)

,
(12)
where ϕX,(d) is the node update MLP for node type X.
Edge Update: The edge gets its attribute updated as:
eY,(d)
ij
= ωX,(d) 
v(d)
i
, eY,(d)
ij
, v(d)
j

, A(eY
ij) = {vi, vj}, (13)
where A(eY
ij) are two end point nodes of the edge eY
ij, ωY,(d)(·)
is the attribute update function for the edge type Y. At
the last layer, say the D-th layer, we set the ReLu as an
activation function for guaranteeing the non-negative power
allocation. The AP activation is determined by the sigmoid
output σ(vX,(D)
m
), where the AP is active if σ(vX,(D)
m
) ≥0.5,
and inactive otherwise. This threshold-based approach enables
a smooth and differentiable decision process.
3) Loss function & model training
To minimize the total power consumption, we define the
loss functions as
L(Ψ) =ϵ1MSE({Pmk}, {P ∗
mk}) + ϵ2BCE(Mact, M∗act)
+
X
m∈Mact
X
k∈K
Pk +
X
k∈K
α(γk −γthr
k )
+ β(bT psen −νpT
senApsen),
(14)
where MSE({Pmk}, {P ∗
mk}) and BCE(Mact, M∗act) denote
the mean squared error and binary cross-entropy, respectively,
comparing the model’s predictions to labels generated by the
modified-BB algorithm. The coefficients ϵ1 and ϵ2 control the
influence of these supervised learning terms, guiding the model
while the remaining unsupervised term seeks an improved
solution. The penalty factors α and β are used for regulating
the loss incurred, when the constraints in (8c) and (8d) are
violated, respectively.
4) Computational Complexity
In this section, we analyze the computational complexity
of the proposed HetGNN model. Following the approach in
[17], we divide the computational cost of GNN-based models
into two main components: (i) the forward pass and (ii)
the backpropagation. The complexity of each component is
examined in the subsequent discussion.
Forward Pass: The computation cost of each step in the
forward pass is divided into four distinct steps as follows.
For clarity, the superscript layer index (l) is omitted.
• Message Generation: We can generalize the message


--- Page 5 ---
5
generation in (11) as ϱ(XW), where W represents the
set of parameters of ψX,(h)(·), and ϱ(·) is a non-linear
activation function. Without loss of generality, assuming
a constant embedding size F across all node feature for
every D HetGNN layers, the computation complexity of
this step is on the order of O((M + K + T)F 2 + (M +
K + T)).
• Message Passing: For each edge e ∈E, two messages
of size F are aggregated, resulting in a total order of
O(2(MK + MT)).
• Node Embedding Update: In (12), applying the MLP
ϕX,(h)(·) yields the same computational complexity as the
first step of O((M + K + T)F 2 + (M + K + T)).
• Edge Embedding Update: In (13), applying the MLP
ωY,(d)(·) to edges yields the computational complexity
on the order of O((MK + KT)F 2 + (MK + KT)).
Backward Pass: In reverse-mode automatic differentiation,
the gradient computation retraces the forward-pass graph.
Therefore, the backward-pass cost matches the forward-pass
cost. Hence, for an L-layer HetGNN with maximum initial
feature dimension F on the HetGraph of K UEs, M APs,
and T SRs, the total computational complexity is given by:
O(4L
␀(M + K + T)F 2 + M(K + T)

).
(15)
Compared to the model-based algorithm in Remark 1, which
relies on multiple iterative loops and high computational
overhead, the proposed HetGNN achieves significantly lower
and more scalable complexity. After training, the model runs
in time linear in the number of UEs, APs and SRs, enabling
rapid and efficient deployment in large-scale ISAC systems.
IV. PERFORMANCE EVALUATION
This section evaluates the performance of the proposed
HetGNN-based framework by numerical results. Regarding the
proposed HetGNN, we use a three-layer model where each
layer employs two MLPs with [input−256−128] architecture
with LeakyReLU activation for message passing and node
updating. The model is trained on various generated scenarios
and evaluation on unseen data. For comparison, we consider
the following benchmarks: Decoupled method: We incre-
mentally build the active-AP set, starting from |Mact| = 1.
For each candidate set, we solve the convex subproblem (8)
without the sensing constraint to obtain an initial power
vector ¯p. We then enforce both communication and sensing
requirements via the scaling factor κ = max

bT ¯p
ν ¯pAPA¯p, 1

,
where b and A are given in (7). If any AP exceeds its transmit
power limit, the candidate set is considered infeasible. We
then consider the next set or increment |Mact|, repeating
this procedure until all constraints are satisfied.
Full AP +
Constraint relaxed: The problem in (8) is reformulated as
in Section III-A, with all APs activated (Mact = M), and it
is then solved directly using a convex solver.
Deep Deter-
ministic Policy Gradient (DDPG): We adopt an actor–critic
framework having a pair of Q-networks and one policy net-
work. The actor is a three-layer MLP (128 −64 −output)
with sigmoid activations; the critic is an MLP (64 −128 −1)
applied to the concatenated 1-dimensional (1D) flattened state
and action.

Fig. 4: Averaged number of
active APs versus the number
of UEs when M = 50.
For the network configuration in this study, all APs, SRs,
and UEs are distributed uniformly at random within a cir-
cular area of diameter D = 500 [m], with the static target
positioned at the center of that circle. All the channel gains
obey the uncorrelated Rayleigh fading model. Particularly,
h ∼CN(0, µ), where µn,k is the large-scale fading coefficient
between two devices formulated as µ = FSPL + χ, where
FSPL = −120.9 −37.6 log10(d) [dB] is the free-space path
loss, and the shadow fading χ is generated from a log-
normal Gaussian distribution with standard deviation 7 [dB].
The general system parameters are provided in Table I. In this
work, we train the HetGNN model on the data using multiple
system settings of K = [5, 10, 15, 20] and M = [30, 50, 80].
The supervised learning coefficients are set to ϵ1 = ϵ2 = 0.2,
and the unsupervised loss penalty factors to α = β = 1.
This semi-supervised formulation balances supervised and
unsupervised contributions, resulting in stable convergence and
improved generalization [18].
Fig. 3 illustrates the total power consumption as a function
of the number of UEs, K ∈[5, 20], while relying on M = 50
available APs. The proposed HetGNN model achieves the low-
est power consumption by leveraging a semi-supervised learn-
ing approach, combining insights from the modified-BB along
with the relaxed method using unsupervised self-improvement.
The HetGNN reduces power consumption by 25% compared
to the modified-BB with the relaxed constraint method and by
about 60% compared to when the system activates all APs.
For instance, for 10 UEs, the proposed HetGNN consumes
3 W versus 3.8 W for the modified-BB method. The DDPG
model performs competitively in smaller settings (M = 5),
matching HetGNN. However, due to the 1D flattened input
structure, its performance degrades significantly as the system
dimension escalates. With increasing M, the input dimension
grows rapidly, creating a bottleneck. In larger systems, DDPG
slightly outperforms the modified-BB approach but fails to
match HetGNN. Furthermore, it is important to note that
a separate DDPG model is trained and evaluated for each
scenario. The fully active AP method reduces power compared

6

and model-based approaches with different system scenarios.

K

Run-time [s]

(80.55%)

(100%)

50

(73.93%)
8.69
(77.31%)
8.92
(79.32%)
11.24
(100%)
0.12
0.14
6035.32
2904.19
to the decoupled approach, but remains inefficient due to
unnecessary hardware usage from activating all APs, leading
to higher overall power consumption. The decoupled method
yields the highest power consumption, scaling linearly with
the number of UEs due to its direct allocation strategy. At 20
UEs, it consumes around 11.4 W, about 50% more than the
modified-BB using relaxed constraints.
The average number of active APs is shown in Fig. 4. The
proposed HetGNN activates the fewest APs while supporting
high communication and sensing performance. Specifically,
it requires only 16 APs to serve 20 UEs, while meeting
the sensing requirement, despite using less resources than
the modified BB harnessing the relaxed constraint method,
which activates 32 APs. The DDPG model activates fewer
APs than the baseline methods but still more than HetGNN,
requiring 29 APs for 20 UEs. This reflects its limited ability to
scale efficiently under increasing system size. By contrast, the
decoupled method gradually increases the number of active
APs, while searching for a feasible power allocation. As a
result, it requires around 42 APs to support 20 UEs and
the sensing task. This is almost twice the number used by
HetGNN.
Table II compares both the total power consumption and
runtime across different system settings. The proposed Het-
GNN consistently achieves the lowest power usage, reducing
it by up to 30% compared to the decoupled method and 5−7%
compared to the modified BB. For example, at M = 30
and K = 6, HetGNN consumes only 4.04 W, significantly
lower than the decoupled method’s 4.34 W. Although the
DDPG model outperforms the modified-BB method, it still
consumes about 3 −5% more energy compared to the pro-
posed HetGNN. In terms of runtime, HetGNN processes the
CSI almost instantly (≤0.12 s), while the heuristic-based
approaches require hours when K = 20. At M = 50, K = 20,
HetGNN and DDPG completes execution in 0.12 −0.14 s,
compared to 6035.32 s for the modified BB method using
the relaxed constraints method, highlighting its nearly 10, 000
times faster computation. Unlike heuristic approaches, whose
runtime escalates drastically with the number of APs, the pro-
posed HetGNN and DDPG maintain near-constant execution
time due to its lightweight arithmetic operations. However, an
increase in run-time is observed in the DDPG model due to the
scalability limitations of its 1D flattened input representation.
V. CONCLUSION
The joint AP activation and power control problem of a cell-
free ISAC network was solved by minimizing the total transmit
power, while meeting demanding SINR and CRB-based sens-
ing accuracy requirements. To solve the challenging MINLP
problem, a semi-supervised HetGNN was proposed for effi-
ciently activating the APs and power allocation efficiently. Our
numerical results demonstrated that the HetGNN significantly
outperforms heuristic approaches, despite reducing the power
consumption and promt execution. Future work will extend
this framework to multi-target and multi-user scenarios, further
enhancing both scalability and efficiency.

F. Dong, J. Zhu, Y. Xiong, W. Yuan, Y. Cui, and L. Hanzo, “Integrated
sensing and communications: Recent advances and ten open challenges,”
IEEE Internet of Things J., pp. 1–1, Feb. 2024.

radar and communication spectrum sharing enhancement,” IEEE Trans.
Veh. Technol., vol. 72, no. 2, pp. 2723–2727, 2023.

radar-communications: Information embedding using sidelobe control
and waveform diversity,” IEEE Trans. Signal Process., vol. 64, no. 8,
pp. 2168–2181, 2016.

“Integrated sensing and communications: Toward dual-functional wire-
less networks for 6G and beyond,” IEEE J. Sel. Areas Commun., vol. 40,
no. 6, pp. 1728–1767, 2022.

and communication in cell-free massive MIMO,” IEEE Trans. Wireless
Commun., vol. 23, no. 9, 2024.

for network integrated sensing and communication,” IEEE Trans. Veh.
Technol., vol. 71, no. 12, 2022.

vancements, challenges, and prospects,” IEEE Commun. Mag., vol. 62,
no. 9, pp. 144–150, 2024.

graph neural network design for sum ergodic spectral efficiency max-

1–6, Nov. 2024.

for wireless communications: From theory to practice,” IEEE Trans.

bandwidth and power allocation for multi-homing networks,” IEEE
Trans. Signal Process., vol. 63, no. 7, pp. 1684–1699, 2015.

for target localization in distributed multiple-radar architectures,” IEEE
Trans. Signal Process, vol. 59, no. 7, pp. 3226–3240, 2011.

no. 1, pp. 572–580, 2012.

Hwang, “HGNN: A hierarchical graph neural network architecture for
joint resource management in dynamic wireless sensor networks,” IEEE

supervised learning with consistency and confidence,” in NeurIPS,
